title: NOC 94102 – Glass forming and finishing machine operators and glass cutters | GTR Immigration
description: In 2026, Canada’s glass manufacturing sector is undergoing a high-tech transformation. Driven by the demand for energy-efficient "Smart Glass" in construction and specialized glassware for the booming biotech industry, Glass Machine Operators and Cutters (NOC 94102) are more essential than ever. As facilities shift toward automated precision cutting and sustainable melting processes, international workers with experience in CNC glass-cutting or industrial kiln management are being fast-tracked to meet critical labor shortages in Ontario and Quebec.

classification.teer: FeatureDetailsBroad Occupational Category9 – Occupations in manufacturing and utilitiesTEER Category4 (Usually requiring secondary school and on-the-job training)Major Group94 – Machine operators, assemblers and inspectorsSub-Major Group941 – Processing and manufacturing machine operators

common_job_titles: CNC Glass Cutter / Automatic Glass Cutting Machine Operator
duties: Process Control: Operating multi-function machinery to mix raw materials and melt glass in high-temperature furnaces.
Forming & Moulding: Setting up and adjusting automatic feeding and forming machines that press or blow molten glass into shapes.
Precision Finishing: Operating machines to grind, drill, sand, or polish glass products to exact engineering tolerances.
Quality Verification: Visually inspecting products and using precision gauges to ensure compliance with quality standards.
2026 Update: Monitoring real-time digital sensors for thermal glass tempering to ensure maximum energy-efficiency ratings.
employment_requirements: Education: Completion of secondary school is usually required.
Experience: Previous experience as a machine operator helper in glass manufacturing is typically required for lead operator roles.
Manual Cutting: For hand glass cutters, experience as a helper in a glass-cutting shop is standard.
Certification: Internal safety certifications (WHMIS 2026) and specialized training in handling vacuum-lifting equipment.
job_outlook: The Canadian Occupational Projection System (COPS) expects a balanced market. While automation simplifies routine tasks, it increases the need for workers who can manage complex robotic interfaces. In Ontario, retirement rates are projected to open significant vacancies over the next decade.
labour_market_demand: The 2026 outlook for NOC 94102 is rated as Moderate to Good, specifically in manufacturing hubs:
Ontario (GTA & Mississauga): Good prospects. This region is currently experiencing a labor shortage, with more job openings than available workers to fill them.
Quebec: Stable demand driven by the production of flat glass for the aerospace and automotive sectors.
Alberta: Steady growth in the production of specialized glass for the energy and industrial sectors.
British Columbia: Focused demand for architectural glass cutters to support sustainable high-rise developments.
Why Work in Canada as a Glass Operator?
Technological Advancement: Work with state-of-the-art robotic cutting arms, electric kilns, and digital process control panels.
Diverse Sector Opportunities: Opportunities span across construction (windows), automotive (windshields), and consumer goods (bottles/glassware).
Pathway to Management: Proven operators frequently progress to NOC 92010 (Supervisors), which offers higher earning potential and expanded PR options.
Workplace Safety: Canada maintains world-leading health and safety standards for high-temperature and sharp-material environments.
pathways: Ontario Immigrant Nominee Program (OINP): NOC 94102 is an eligible occupation for the Employer Job Offer: In-Demand Skills Stream.
TR to PR Pathway (New for 2026): In 2026, Canada is launching a dedicated stream to transition up to 33,000 temporary workers with essential skills into permanent residents.
Rural and Northern Immigration Pilot (RNIP): A high-success pathway for those willing to work in smaller manufacturing communities in BC and Ontario.
Provincial Nominee Programs (PNP): Look for Semi-Skilled or Entry-Level streams in Alberta and British Columbia.
